coredata.dc:title: One-step green aqueous synthesis of blue light emitting copper nanoclusters for quantitative determination of food color Ponceau 4R
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Er Demirhan, Buket
Şatana Kara, Hayriye Eda
Demirhan, Burak
coredata.dc:description: Depending on the increase of food safety awareness, the quality control of artificial food color Ponceau 4R gain significant due to the adverse effect of dye on human health. For this reason, sensitive, selective, precise, and low cost methods must be developed to meet to need. The copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) were synthesized based on the reaction of copper and L‐cysteine as a reducing and stabilizing agent by the one-pot hydrothermal process and characterized with different methods. The effect of pH, incubation time, and temperature on emission signals of CuNCs were evaluated. The addition of Ponceau 4R into nanocluster solution induced the quenching of fluorescence and possible quenching mechanism was static quenching. Under the optimum conditions, the linearity was achieved between 1.5 and 30 μg mL−1. The selectivity and sensitivity studies showed that developed sensor has excellent selectivity for Ponceau 4R and high sensitivity with low LOD value, 0.028 μg mL−1. The developed fluorescent CuNCs sensor allows to determine of Ponceau 4R in powder drinks, candy, sugar paste, and pharmaceutical syrups samples detection with a high recovery value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanocluster
Ponceau 4R
Fluorescence sensor
Food additives

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of L‐cysteine capped copper nanoclusters
Interaction between Ponceau 4R and copper nanoclusters
Characterization of synthesized L‐cysteine capped copper nanoclusters
Fluorescence detection of Ponceau 4R by CuNCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CuNCs, (B) Dynamic light scattering a- CuNCs and b- CuNCs-Ponceau 4R mixture (C)FTIR spectrum a- L-cysteine and b-CuNCs (D) Raman spectra of CuNCs (red line), Ponceau 4R (blue line), and mixture (gray line) (E) a- The survey XPS spectrum of L-csyteine capped CuNCs and b-XPS spectrum of Cu in CuNCs.
Absorption (dashed line), excitation (line), and emission (dotted line) spectra of CuNC.
(A) Absorption spectra of Cu(NO3)2 (a), L-cysteine (b), and CuNCs (c), (B) Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs (a), L-cysteine (b), and Cu(NO3)2 (c).
Optimization conditions for synthesis and experimental; (A) L-cysteine quantity (125- 225 mg), (B) incubation time (0-300 min), (C) incubation temperature (25-65 °C), and (D) effect of pH (2-11).
Effect of various interfering substances on the spectra (A) and the (F0-F)/ F0 of the system (B) (Concentration of Ponceau 4R was 50 μg mL−1 and interfering substances were 2500 μg mL−1).

Ponceau 4R concentration dependent fluorescence emission of the CuNCs. Ponceau 4R concentration 2.5-50 μM in pH 6 phosphate buffer (dotted line is QDs alone). The inset shows the change of the fluorescence intensity with the increase of the Ponceau 4R concentration.
Fluorescence decay curves of CuNCs in absence and presence of 4.5 μg mL−1 Ponceau 4R.
Statistical evaluation of calibration data of Ponceau 4R detected by the CuNCs as a fluorescent probe.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SE is the standard error, RSD is the relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of samples and recovery analysis of Ponceau 4R.
Comparison of the proposed method with different techniques reported for the determination of Ponceau 4R.

head.ce:title: One-step green aqueous synthesis of blue light emitting copper nanoclusters for quantitative determination of food color Ponceau 4R

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Basic Sciences, Faculty of Pharmacy, Gazi University, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Basic Sciences, Faculty of Pharmacy, Gazi University, Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Gazi University, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Gazi University, Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Depending on the increase of food safety awareness, the quality control of artificial food color Ponceau 4R gain significant due to the adverse effect of dye on human health. For this reason, sensitive, selective, precise, and low cost methods must be developed to meet to need. The copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) were synthesized based on the reaction of copper and L‐cysteine as a reducing and stabilizing agent by the one-pot hydrothermal process and characterized with different methods. The effect of pH, incubation time, and temperature on emission signals of CuNCs were evaluated. The addition of Ponceau 4R into nanocluster solution induced the quenching of fluorescence and possible quenching mechanism was static quenching. Under the optimum conditions, the linearity was achieved between 1.5 and 30 μg mL−1. The selectivity and sensitivity studies showed that developed sensor has excellent selectivity for Ponceau 4R and high sensitivity with low LOD value, 0.028 μg mL−1. The developed fluorescent CuNCs sensor allows to determine of Ponceau 4R in powder drinks, candy, sugar paste, and pharmaceutical syrups samples detection with a high recovery value.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic organic dyes are used in the food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industry [1]. Azo dyes that contain two aromatic rings connected by an NN double bond, are a class of synthetic organic dyes [2,3]. Ponceau 4R, trisodium;7-hydroxy-8-[(4-sulfonatonaphthalen-1- yl)diazenyl]naphthalene-1,3-disulfonate, is a synthetic azo dye and gives a red color also known as E124, New Coccine, Coccine Red, Food Red, or Acid Red [3,4]. As a colorant Ponceau 4R is used in drinks such as fruit juice, carbonated drinks, wine, and sweets, candies, ice cream, confectionery, cakes, and dairy products like yogurt, etc., because of its advantages such as effective coloring, good color stability, high resolution, and low cost [5,6]. In addition, Ponceau 4R is widely preferable as a coloring material in medicines, tobacco and cosmetic industries in many countries [7,8]. Although Ponceau 4R is allowed to be added to different foods in some countries [9], indiscriminate use of this synthetic dye is also not safe [10]. Research results have shown that a high level consumption of Ponceau 4R can cause adverse health effects. Some studies have confirmed that metabolites of Ponceau 4R (β-naphthyl amine and α-amino-1-naphthol) lead to severe health problems such as adverse desensitization, cytotoxicity, carcinogenicity and mutagenicity [9]. In addition to these, dietary consumption of Ponceau 4R affects children's behavior, causing increased hyperactivity and intolerance [11]. Therefore, the usage of Ponceau 4R is strictly controlled with legal regulations [12]. Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) of Ponceau 4R is determined as 0.7 mg kg−1 body weight (bw) per day by The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) [13]. The permitted maximum Ponceau 4R levels in foods and beverages are indicated in the Turkish Food Codex [14].

Consequently, reliable, sensitive, and rapid analytical methods for the determination of Ponceau 4R are needed for the confidence of food safety [15]. For this purpose high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with diode array detector (DAD) [16], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [17], mass spectrometry (MS) [18], Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [19], spectrophotometer [5], electrochemical techniques using different electrode systems [20–22], and polarography [23] methods have been developed. Although mentioned methods are proven and commonly used, factors such as high cost equipment, need for expert staff, low repeatability, extensive and time-consuming sample preparation are cited as disadvantages of these methods. Therefore, it is essential to develop a sensitive, repeatable, easy-to-apply, time-saving, and cost-effective, method for quantitative determination of Ponceau 4R [6].

In the last two decades, various fluorescent nanomaterials such as fluorescent dyes, carbon dots quantum dots, organic-inorganic hybrid nanomaterials, and metal nanoclusters (NCs) have been discovered and found wide usage in research area areas including pharmaceutical analysis, bioimaging, and electronics [24]. Fluorescent metal nanoclusters which are molecular aggregates forms of few to about 100 metal atoms and their size is typically less than 2 nm. These sub-nanometer nanoclusters act as bridges between atoms and nanoparticles and they exhibit molecular-like properties such as the HOMO-LUMO transition, stereochemical activity, redox property, and intrinsic magnetism [25,26]. In addition, nanoclusters have been great interest in the research area due to their advantages such as high Stokes shift, high quantum efficiency, ultrasmall particle size, light stability, catalytic properties, tunable fluorescence emission, low toxicity, water solubility, and biocompatibility [27,28]. These properties allow being suitable candidates as fluorescent probes of nanoclusters. Because of these advantages, nanoclusters have been used as sensors for the analysis of food [29,30], pharmaceuticals [31,32], nucleic acids [33], proteins [34], and etc. These studies are based on changing of luminescence properties of nanoclusters such as emission spectrum and lifetime due to interaction of analyte and surface of nanoclusters. In these studies, used nanoclusters were generally gold, silver, and copper. Among them, copper nanoclusters as novel fluorescent probes are a better candidate for different analytical applications due to their great fluorescence properties, good water solubility, ease of synthesis and usage. In addition, copper nanoclusters are cheaper than gold and silver, and this allows to be synthesized at less cost. Different methods are used for the synthesis of copper nanoclusters, such as chemical reduction, photoreduction sonochemical, microwave, and chemical etching techniques. However, some of these methods needed complicated instruments, preparations, high cost, and harmful chemicals. Therefore, development of simple and eco-friendly methodologies for synthesize water soluble, stable, and high effective NCs without using strong chemicals is necessary.

In this paper, the fluorescent blue emitting L‐cysteine stabilized Cu nanoclusters (CuNCs) were synthesized in aqueous solution without requiring additional stabilizer, organic agents and strong reducing agents to determine the artificial food color Ponceau 4R as a fluorescent probe. Herein, a new method was developed through using the CuNCs as fluorescent signal substance. The proposed method suitable for green chemistry, and mentioned CuNCs have stable fluorescence intensity. After characterization of synthesized CuNCs with spectroscopic techniques such as UV–vis, fluorescence, and FT-IR, TEM, XPS, Raman spectroscopy, zeta potential measurement and dynamic light scattering analyses, optimum experimental parameters were identified and developed fluorescent sensor was applied to food samples and pharmaceutical syrups. Although there were two studies based on using CdTe quantum dots [6] and graphene quantum dots [15] in the literature, to our knowledge, the determination methods of Ponceau 4R in different food and especially pharmaceutical samples based on using CuNCs has not been reported, in this field the proposed method is novel. The proposed method is not only very sensitive with low detection limit, repeatable, simple, fast, inexpensive, and environmental friendly but also can help to protect the safety of foods and the rights of consumers.

Ponceau 4R, methylene blue, and erythrosine were purchased from Roha Dye Chem. Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India), quinoline yellow was obtained from Williams Ltd. (Houslow, UK). L-cysteine hydrochloride hydrate (99.0 %) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Switzerland). Copper nitrate, sodium hydroxide, phosphoric acid, ascorbic acid, citric acid, glucose, sucrose, sodium citrate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride and magnesium chloride were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). In the current study, a total of four food samples (2 powder drinks, 1 candy, and 1 sugar paste) were collected from local markets in Ankara, Turkey. The syrup samples of three different brands were obtained from pharmacies in Ankara, Turkey. All samples were kept at room temperature and analyzed as soon as possible. All the other solvents and chemicals used were of analytical grade. Stock solutions of Ponceau 4R (1.0 × 10−3 mol L-1) were prepared in deionized water and kept in the refrigerator.

Sodium hydroxide (0.4 M) and phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 2.00, 3.00, 6.00–8,00, and 11.00) were prepared in deionized water and pH was adjusted using sodium hydroxide (5 M). All buffer solutions were prepared using analytical grade reagents and deionized water. Water used throughout was deionized water (>18 MΩ. cm).

Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorimeter was used for the fluorescence measurements equipped with a Xenon lamp and 10 × 10 mm path length quartz cells at room temperature with excitation and emission slit at 10.0 nm. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a Specord 50 Plus (Analytik Jena, Germany) with quartz carrier. TEM images were obtained using FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit Biotwin CTEM. FTIR studies were carried out on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400 FTIR/FTNIR spectrometer equipped with a Universal ATR Sampling Accessory (Perkin Elmer Inc., Waltham, Ma, USA) and were reported in cm−1 units. Zetasizer Nano ZS Series, Malvern instrument was used for Zeta potential and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis. Fluorescence lifetime was obtained using Horiba Fluorolog 3 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific, USA) equipped with 390 nm nanoled. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were done by using PHI 5000 VersaProbe III multitechnique XPS (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Raman measurements were performed using a Raman Microscopy (Deltanu Inc., Laramie, WY, USA). The pH measurements were performed using a combined pH electrode with a Mettler-Toledo GmbH (Greifensee- Switzerland) pH meter. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of L‐cysteine capped copper nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: L‐Cysteine capped CuNCs were synthesized by minor modification in aqueous solutions according to a reported method [35]. Briefly, 5 mL 0.4 M NaOH solution and 175 mg L‐cysteine were added into the beaker and stirred by magnetic stirrer until dissolved. After, 500 μL 1.0 mM solution of Cu(NO3)2 was added drop by drop to the above solution and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. After this step, the solution was heated for 4 h at 55 °C. At the end of the heating, the slight yellow color of the solution turns to dark yellow. The obtained nanocluster solution was kept at 4 °C and was stable for up to 6 months when stored under this temperature.

The procedure of extraction Ponceau 4R from the samples as follows; 1 g of powdered candies/ powder drinks samples/sugar paste or 1 mL syrups were taken and dissolved in 10 mL 0.1 M pH 6 phosphate buffer solution. After extraction, to separate the undissolved contents, sample solutions were centrifuged. Fifty microliters of these extracts were used for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between Ponceau 4R and copper nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate fluorescence-quenching effect, a ten microliters CuNC solution and different volumes of standard Ponceau 4R solution or sample solution were put into a quartz fluorescent cuvette and total volume made up to 2 mL by adding 0.1 M pH 6 phosphate buffer. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with the excitation and emission wavelengths of 375 and 470 nm in the presence and absence of Ponceau 4R. The quenching of fluorescence intensity was recorded as a ratio F0 / F, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the probe in the absence or presence of Ponceau 4R. All measurements were carried out three times and obtained average values were used for the calculations.

To investigate the selectivity of CuNCs toward Ponceau 4R, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs was measured in the presence of possible interfering substances such as glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, sucrose, sodium citrate, erythrosine, methylene blue, and quinoline yellow and some common ions including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+. In this study the concentrations of Ponceau 4R and interferents were 50 μg mL−1 and 2500 μg mL−1, respectively. In this study, the change in fluorescence emission values of nanoclusters was observed by adding both Ponceau 4R and interfering species separately on CNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized L‐cysteine capped copper nanoclusters
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and optical properties of synthesized CuNCs were identified by using different spectroscopic techniques such as UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), zeta potential, and dynamic light scattering analyses, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), XPS, and Raman spectroscopy. The TEM image showed that the CuNCs had a spherical shape, their size was at about 3 nm and the particle size distribution was uniform (Fig. 1 A). The average particle size of these CuNCs were found as 2.8 nm with using TEM imaging method. The results of dynamic light scattering analysis indicated that the diameter of CuNCs was 3.2 nm which agreement with the particle size obtained from TEM analysis (Fig. 1 B). The slightly larger size shown in the DLS analysis is due to the hydrodynamic radius. On the other hand, it is seen that CuNCs have a higher zeta potential value −20.4 mV at the zeta potential analysis in its measured aqueous solution (pH at around 9), which means synthesized particles were negatively charged and it showed that this situation protects the nanoclusters from aggregation. All these suggest the successful preparation of CuNCs.

To identify the surface groups of obtained CuNCs, the spectra of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded. When compared the IR spectra of CuNCs and L-cysteine, the band at 2561.6 cm−1 corresponding to a − SH stretching vibration was disappeared which explained the covalent bond formation between sulfur atoms of L‐cysteine and Cu atoms on the surface of the NCs (Fig. 1C). The FT-IR analysis indicated that the surface of CuNCs capped with L‐cysteine. Raman spectra were used to assist the FTIR spectra with analyzing the structure of substances (Fig. 1D). As seen from figure L-cysteine capped CuNCs showed four bands at 440, 693, 1120, and 1278 cm−1.

The XPS wide spectrum of the CuNCs illustrated that the CuNCs were composed of all the expected elements including C, O, N, S, and Cu (Fig. 1E). On the total XPS spectrum of CuNCs, five peaks appeared at 162.0, 284.6, 398.4, 530.9 and 932.2 eV which were assigned to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cu 2p state. In the present study, The Cu2p spectrum showed two peaks at 952.2 and 932.2 eV representing Cu 2p1/2 and 2p3/2, respectively, according well with Cu (0) state (Fig. 1E). Besides, the absence of a satellite peak around 942 eV in the XPS narrow spectrum of Cu suggested the lack of Cu2+ in the CuNCs (Fig. 1E).

From the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the synthesized nanoclusters have an absorption peak at 375 nm. Excitation and emission spectra showed that a strong blue emission centered at 470 nm when excited at 375 nm (Fig. 2 ). The quantum yield (PL-QY) of synthesized CuNCs was found as 13.8 % by using the quinine reagent as a reference standard. The stability of prepared nanoclusters was stable for at least 2 months in water without remarkable precipitation in the darkness at 4 °C.

Subsequently, absorption and emission spectra of L-cysteine, Cu(NO3)2, and CuNCs were compared in Fig. 3 respectively. As seen from the figure, CuNCs have a weak absorption peak at around 375 nm, while both L-cysteine and Cu(NO3)2 exhibited no obvious peaks (Fig. 3A). Similarly, when fluorescence spectra were examined, it is seen that the fluorescence emission was caused by CuNCs while no signal was observed from other molecules (Fig. 3B). Overall results suggest the successful preparation of CuNCs and synthesized particles could be used as a fluorescent sensor.

In this work, CuNCs, in which L-cysteine served as both a reducing and a protection agent, were synthesized. In order to identify the optimum synthesis conditions of CuNCs, the effect of reaction time, temperature, and concentration of L-cysteine were examined (Fig. 4 ). In the first step, to determine optimum L-cysteine concentration, 125 mg, 150 mg, 175 mg, 200 mg, and 225 mg L-cysteine quantity were tried. The increased addition of L-cysteine would raise the reduction rate of Cu2+, which would lead to formation of large nanoclusters. When the amount of L-cysteine was too small, the degree of reduction was incomplete, which would lead to low stability and an agglomeration of CuNCs. According to the obtained experimental results, emission signal increased up to 175 mg and after this concentration, decreased (Fig. 4A). The mixing time of nanoclusters also had a great influence on the fluorescence properties of the final CuNCs. Hence, secondly, the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs at 0 min, 60 min, 120 min, 180 min, 240 min, and 300 min were tested. The obtained results showed that maximum signal intensity was achieved at 240 min, after this time the emission signal of CuNCs decreased. The longer incubation time was good for the reduction reaction and better structure of nanoclusters. When the mixing time exceeded the optimum point, the fluorescence intensity decreased, which might be due to the uneven etching of CuNCs due to excessive growth or larger aggregation, which led to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. The experimental results showed that the color of CuNCs turned to dark brown and precipitation occurred (Fig. 4B). Finally, the effect of temperature on the fluorescence signal was investigated at room temperature, 35 °C, 45 °C, 55 °C, and 65°C. The number of obtained Cu nanoclusters was limited at lower temperature, while CuNCs grew too fast at higher temperature. Similarly of incubation time study, at high temperature, the dark brown precipitate formed. Both of them could not be suitable to the expected fluorescence performance of CuNCs. The highest emission signal achieved at 55 °C this temperature was selected (Fig. 4C). As a result, the synthesis of CuNCs depends on experimental conditions, and thus, 175 mg L-cysteine, 240 min, and 55 °C were selected as optimum conditions.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of CuNCs was studied. As seen from the Fig. 4D, the fluorescence emission intensities of CuNCs was dependent on pH changes. At the acidic area, the fluorescence intensities at 470 nm were low because high proton concentrations caused to the neutralization of negatively charged CuNCs. However, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing of pH to 6. This pH-dependent fluorescence feature may be due to the change in the surface charge of CuNCs due to protonation. Some decrease in emission signal after pH 6 was observed. The quenched phosphorescence intensity was also changed with the pH and shown similar behaviour. Meanwhile, the stability of Ponceau 4R is most stable at pH 6–8. Hence, another factor in the selection of the pH value was that the color of the dye turns to brown in an alkaline medium, so, it loses its stability. Finally, pH 6 was selected as an experimental pH condition.

The effect of reaction time from 0 to 5 min on fluorescence intensity was also identified and there was no obvious variation after 1 min. In order to develop and apply this method to the samples, reaction time was chosen as 1 min as the experimental conditions.

The aim of this study was to determine the Ponceau 4R concentration in different types of food samples such as candy, powder drinks, sugar paste, and pharmaceutical syrups. In order to identify the selectivity of the fluorescent sensor, different substances, which have similar structural molecules or were coexisting or same colored food coloring, were chosen and their effect on the CuNCs fluorescence emission intensity was studied.

For this purpose, potentially interfering components including glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, sucrose, sodium citrate, erythrosine, methylene blue, quinoline yellow, and some common ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+ were tested. Fig. 5 showed that change in fluorescence intensity of CuNCs upon the addition of Ponceau 4R and possible interfering substances. Synthesized CuNCs have high selectivity towards Ponceau 4R by giving a high value of (F0-F) / F0. These possible interfering molecules did not show any interference even up to 50-fold excess of the concentration of Ponceau 4R at 50 μg mL−1.

CuNCs were chosen to be the fluorescent probe for their excellent properties such as high efficient and stable emission signal, low toxicity, and simple synthesis procedure. CuNCs surface has a carboxyl group and Ponceau 4R possesses three sulfonic groups. Both sulfonic groups and the oxygen atoms on the surface of the carboxyl groups are electronegative, therefore, these interactions cause to form a complex between Ponceau 4R and CuNCs. The Raman spectra indicated that complex formation of dye and CuNCs (Fig. 1D). It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs was effectively quenched by Ponceau 4R due to the interaction of these two groups and the forming of hydrogen bonds [15] Complex molecules formed between fluorophore and quencher changed the fluorescence character [36–38]. As seen from Fig. 6, with the increasing concentration of Ponceau 4R, the fluorescent intensity of CuNCs decreased clearly, which pointed out that Ponceau 4R has a remarkable quenching effect on the fluorescence emission of CuNCs. In addition, a blue shift was observed in the emission wavelength of CuNCs, indicated that the size of quantum dots changed. This situation was showed with the dynamic light scattering results, which showed that the size of synthesized CuNCs was 3.2 nm, while after reaction with dye it was 17.4 nm (Fig. 1B).

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching processes could be divided into two ways as dynamic (collisional) and static quenching. In both processes, the quencher and the fluorophore should be in contact. In the static system, the quencher and fluorophore interact at the ground state and the nonfluorescent complex occurs. For the dynamic or collisional quenching process, the molecules are in contact in the excited state and nonradiative relaxation occurs.

The fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be described Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)), respectively [39,40]
(1) F0 / F = 1 + Kapp [Q]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, Kapp is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, respectively. Kapp is determined by the linear relationship between F0/F and [Q].

The linear curve was F0 / F = 0.032 [Q] + 0.91 with a regression coefficient of r = 0.995 in the concentration range of 2.5–50 μM (Fig. 6 inset). Obtained results showed that, fluorescence quenching mechanism of Ponceau 4R-CuNCs system was static and KSV was found to be 3.2×105 M−1 which means interaction between dye and CuNCs was strong. The results indicated that the developed method allows it to be used as a probe for the determination of Ponceau 4R in samples.

The fluorescence lifetimes measurement is the most definitive method to identify the quenching mechanism. For this purpose, the average lifetime of the fluorophore is measured in the absence and the presence of the quencher. As mentioned above, nonfluorescent complex molecule formed from fluorophore and quencher and the fluorescence emission is due to uncomplexed fluorophores. The uncomplexed fluorophore is stable, and the ratio of lifetimes ratio should be τ0/τ = 1 for static quenching, where τ0 and τ are the lifetime of fluorophore in the absence and the presence of quencher, respectively [40]. In this study, with the addition of Ponceau 4R, the lifetime measurement of CuNCs changed from 7.04 ns to 6.87 ns which is negligible (Fig. 7 ). The value of τ0/τ was calculated as 1.02 which close to 1 and a possible quenching mechanism was recommended static.

Moreover, Kq, indicates the dynamic fluorescence quenching rate constant and is calculated from the Eq. (2)
(2) Kq = KD/τ0
where τ0 is the lifetime of the fluorophore in the absence of quencher. From the fluorescence decay curve, the average fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs was calculated as 7.04 as mentioned above. A Kq value was found as 4.54×1013 mol L−1 s−1 which greater than the maximum dynamic quenching constant 2×1010 mol L−1 s−1 [15]. This result, also, proves that the quenching mechanism is static. The number of binding sites (n) of Ponceau 4R with CuNCs can be calculated using Eq. (3).
(3) log ((F0 / F)/F)= log K + n log [Q]
where K is the binding constant and n is the number of the binding sites, which can be determined by the intercept and slope of logarithm regression curve of log(F0–F)/F versus log[Q], respectively. The n and K were found as 1.2 and 2.5×106 L mol−1, respectively. The high binding constant indicated that Ponceau 4R has a high affinity to CuNCs.

Zeta potential defines the extent of repulsion between the similar charges present on the particle surface. This force inhibits the aggregation and increases the stability of particles. The zeta potential of as synthesized particles has been measured -20,4 mV in aqueous solution (pH around 9) while −11 mV in pH 6 buffer solution. This potential value reduced to −5.3 mV in the presence of Ponceau 4R, which might be shown the aggregation of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Ponceau 4R by CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Ponceau 4R is a synthetic colorant which belongs to the family of azo dyes that may be used as a food coloring. It is stable to light and heat, therefore, it is widely used in a variety of food products. On the other hand, there are opinions related that high consumption of Ponceau 4R has negative effects on the nervous system and reproductive system and may cause cancer. Therefore, its use has been limited and quantification has gained importance. As a result of the interaction of the Ponceau 4R and CuNCs, there is a quenching effect in the emission intensity and this decreasing depends on the concentration of the dye. Therefore, CuNCs have been used as fluorescent probes for the determination of the Ponceau 4R.

The developed fluorescent sensor was studied by adding different concentrations of Ponceau 4R under the optimal conditions. The emission fluorescence intensity of CuNCs was measured at 470 nm when excited at 375 nm. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity was decreasing gradually with the addition of Ponceau 4R because of the interaction between CuNCs and dye. The linearity of the developed fluorescent probe was performed between 1.5–30 μg mL−1 Ponceau 4R concentrations. The developed sensor demonstrated that a good linear correlation was achieved between Ponceau 4R concentration and intensity with an R2 value of 0.998. The linear regression equation was ΔP = 0.05 C + 0.90, where C is the concentration of Ponceau 4R (μg mL−1) and ΔP is the fluorescence quenching intensity (F0/F) (Table 1 ). Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values were calculated by using equations 3 s/m and 10 s/m, where s is standard deviation and m is the slope of calibration graph, respectively. LOD and LOQ values were found as 0.028 μg mL−1 and 0.093 μg mL−1, respectively. Obtained results showed that developed fluorescent probe was reliable and applicable.

The repeatability of the proposed method was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of five replicates on the same day (intra-day precision) and within three consecutive days (inter-day precision). An acceptable precision was obtained with percentage relative standard deviation (RSD %) values as 0.37 % for intra-day and 0.42 % for inter-day experiments (Table 1).

In order to test the accuracy and reproducibility of the proposed method, recovery studies were performed by spiking samples with an appropriate amount of the stock solution of Ponceau 4R. The recovery values were calculated using the related regression equation with a mean value of 3 measurements. The recovery values were in an acceptable range of 98–104 %. Ponceau 4R levels of powder drinks, candy and sugar paste, and pharmaceutical syrups were determined between 65.62–123.21 mg kg−1, and 38.4–95.9 μg mL−1, respectively (Table 2 ).

Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations allows the limits for Ponceau 4R 300 mg kg−1 for hard and soft candy and 500 mg kg−1 for decorations. The proposed method has enough sensitivity for determination the allowed quantity. In addition, when compared with the previous literature, the proposed method showed a wide linear range and low LOD value (Table 3 ). Besides, this method also has advantages such as sensitivity, simplicity, environmentally friendly, and time-saving.

A new specific method for quantitative analysis of Ponceau 4R in candy, powder drinks, sugar paste, and pharmaceutical syrups has been developed based on the using of CuNCs as a fluorescence probe. The interaction between CuNCs and Ponceau 4R caused quenching of fluorescence intensity, and this linear decrease of emission obtained in the range of 1.5–30 μg mL−1 Ponceau 4R concentration. In order to identify the quenching mechanism, fluorescence data were used for Stern-Volmer equation and the quenching constant was calculated. In addition, fluorescence lifetime was also examined. Obtained results indicate that the static quenching mechanism was dominant for the fluorescence quenching of CuNCs. Moreover, the developed method was successfully applied to the quantitative determination of Ponceau 4R in different types of samples. Compared with previous studies, the proposed method was simple, fast, cost effective, sensitive, and suitable for applications in other types of samples.

Buket Er Demirhan: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Hayriye Eda Şatana Kara: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Burak Demirhan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.
